abstract: Organic anion transporting polypeptide 1B1 (OATP1B1) is a critical multispeciﬁc hepatic transporter responsible for the uptake of a wide range of endogenous compounds and clinical drugs, including bile salts, bilirubin, and statins. Despite its central role in drug pharmacokinetics and drug–drug interactions (DDIs), the structural basis of its broad substrate recognition and the mechanics of its transport cycle have remained poorly understood. In this study, we present cryo-EM structures of human OATP1B1 in both outward- and inward-open states, captured in its apo form and in complex with representative ligands: the endogenous metabolites bilirubin and estrone-3-sulfate, the antiviral drug simeprevir, and the fluorescent probe 2′,7′-dichlorofluorescein. These structures identify a central translocation path characterized by an orthosteric binding hub of conserved aromatic residues, supplemented by distinct major and minor pockets that accommodate diverse chemical scaffolds. Our findings reveal that OATP1B1 facilitates transport through a rocker-switch mechanism involving significant conformational rearrangements of the transmembrane domains. Integration of these structural data with molecular dynamics simulations and mutagenesis functional assays comprehensively elucidates the molecular logic behind OATP1B1 polyspecificity. This work provides a definitive structural framework for understanding the OATP family and offers a template for predicting DDIs and optimizing drug delivery to the liver.
